name: Beef Stroganoff
steps: Sear the beef 3-4 minutes.
Stir in sour cream, simmer 3 minutes.
Serve over cooked noodles.
steps_detailed: Heat a splash of oil in a skillet over high heat. Sear the beef in a single layer, 2-3 minutes per side, until browned. Remove.
Add the onion and garlic to the same pan, cooking 5-6 minutes until softened and lightly golden.
Add the broth and mustard, scraping up any browned bits from the pan.
Reduce heat to low, stir in the sour cream, and return the beef to the pan. Warm through gently - don't let it boil, or the sour cream can separate.
Cook the noodles per package directions and serve the stroganoff over them, topped with parsley.
description: Beef stroganoff, thin-sliced beef in a sour-cream sauce, named for the Stroganov family and now a genuine global export of Russian cooking.

name: Chicken Kiev-Style
steps: Spread butter over chicken, roll up tightly.
Coat in breadcrumbs, bake at 400F for 22-25 minutes until cooked through.
steps_detailed: Mix the softened butter with garlic and parsley - this herbed butter is the whole point of the dish, meant to burst out warm and fragrant when cut into.
Pound the chicken breast thin and place a spoonful of the herb butter in the center. Roll up tightly, tucking in the ends to seal the butter inside completely.
Dredge in flour, dip in beaten egg, then coat thoroughly in panko.
Bake at 400F for 22-25 minutes until golden and cooked through (165F internal).
Rest a few minutes before cutting - the butter inside will be very hot.
description: Chicken Kiev, a butter-filled cutlet meant to release a rush of garlic butter when cut into - despite the name, it's disputed whether the dish originated in Kyiv or was invented later by Russian chefs; either way it became a staple of Soviet-era restaurant menus.

name: Beef and Cabbage Stew (Russian)
steps: Brown the beef, 5-6 minutes.
Add cabbage and tomatoes with 1/2 cup water, simmer 20 minutes.
steps_detailed: Heat a splash of oil in a pot over medium-high heat. Brown the beef, breaking it apart, 5-6 minutes.
Add the onion, carrot, and garlic, cooking 5-6 minutes until softened.
Add the tomatoes, broth, and bay leaf. Bring to a simmer.
Add the cabbage, cover, and simmer 20-25 minutes until fully tender.
Remove the bay leaf and serve with a dollop of sour cream - this is 'shchi's heartier cousin, a filling everyday stew.
description: A simple beef and cabbage stew.

name: Beet Soup (Borscht)
steps: Brown the beef, 5 minutes.
Add beets, cabbage, and 2 cups water, simmer 25-30 minutes until beets are tender.
steps_detailed: Heat a splash of oil in a pot over medium-high heat. Brown the beef, breaking it apart, 5-6 minutes.
Add the onion, carrot, and garlic, cooking 5 minutes.
Add the beets, tomatoes, and broth. Bring to a simmer.
Cover and cook 20-25 minutes until the beets are almost tender.
Add the cabbage and simmer 8-10 more minutes until everything is tender - the soup should be a deep, vivid red from the beets.
Serve with a generous dollop of sour cream and a scatter of fresh dill.
description: Borscht, beet soup finished with a dollop of sour cream, is claimed by and beloved across many countries in the region, Russia among them.

name: Pork and Sauerkraut
steps: Brown the pork, 5-6 minutes.
Add sauerkraut and potato with 1/2 cup water, simmer 20 minutes.
steps_detailed: Heat a splash of oil in a pan over medium-high heat. Brown the pork, 5-6 minutes. Remove.
Add the onion, cooking 5 minutes until softened.
Add the caraway seed, sauerkraut, potato, and broth.
Return the pork to the pan, cover, and simmer 20-25 minutes until the potato is tender.
Top with dill before serving - the tang of sauerkraut against rich pork is a defining combination across Russian and Eastern European cooking.
description: A simple pork and sauerkraut dish.

name: Chicken and Rice (Plov-Style)
steps: Brown the chicken, 5-6 minutes.
Add carrot, rice, and 1 cup water, cook 20 minutes.

ingredients_full.note: tbsp, minced whole cloves if possible

steps_detailed: Heat a splash of oil in a heavy pot over medium-high heat and brown the chicken, 5-6 minutes. Remove.
Add the onion, cooking 5-6 minutes until golden, then the carrot, cooking 4-5 minutes more.
Add the garlic and cumin, cooking 1 minute.
Stir in the rice, coating it in the fat and aromatics, then add the broth and raisins.
Nestle the chicken back in, bring to a boil, reduce heat, cover, and cook 20-25 minutes until the rice is tender - plov is beloved across Russia and Central Asia, with countless regional variations.
description: Plov, rice cooked with meat, found across Russia as well as Central Asia given their shared history.

name: Beef and Potato Pirozhki-Style Bake
steps: Brown the beef, mix with mashed potato.
Make a simple dough, fill, and bake at 375F for 20 minutes until golden.

ingredients_full.note: beaten, for the dough and egg wash

steps_detailed: Brown the beef with onion, 6-7 minutes, then mix with the mashed potato and butter.
Make a simple dough with the flour, egg, and a splash of water, kneading until smooth.
Roll out and cut into rounds, fill each with the beef-potato mixture, fold over, and seal the edges.
Brush with a little more beaten egg.
Bake at 375F for 20-25 minutes until golden - a baked, home-style take on pirozhki, the beloved stuffed pastries found at every Russian bakery.
description: Pirozhki, small stuffed pastries, given a baked casserole-style treatment here.

name: Beef Salad (Russian Winter Salad Style)
steps: Toss diced beef and potato with sour cream.
steps_detailed: Whisk the sour cream and mustard together for the dressing.
Toss the beef, potato, and carrot with the dressing.
Fold in the dill just before serving - a heartier, protein-forward take on the classic diced-vegetable salads that are a fixture of Russian home cooking.
description: In the style of Olivier salad, a Russian potato-and-meat salad staple, especially for New Year's.

name: Chicken and Buckwheat Bowl
steps: Cook onion until golden, 6-7 minutes.
Serve chicken and onion over cooked rice.

ingredients_full.note: substitute for buckwheat groats (kasha), if unavailable

steps_detailed: Melt the butter in a pan over medium heat and cook the onion 8-10 minutes, stirring often, until deeply golden and sweet.
Cook the rice (or buckwheat groats, if you have them - kasha is the traditional base for this dish) per package directions.
Serve the chicken over the grain, topped with the caramelized onion and dill.
description: Buckwheat (grechka), a Russian pantry staple, paired here with chicken.

name: Dumpling Soup (Pelmeni-Style)
steps: Fill wrappers with seasoned beef, pinch closed.
Simmer in broth 8-10 minutes.
steps_detailed: Mix the beef with onion and garlic.
Fill each wrapper with a small spoonful of filling, fold and pinch tightly closed - pelmeni are traditionally smaller and more delicate than many other dumplings.
Bring the broth to a simmer. Drop the dumplings in and cook 8-10 minutes until they float and the filling is cooked through.
Serve in the broth, topped with sour cream and dill.
description: Pelmeni, small meat dumplings, a Russian and broader Slavic staple, traditionally made in large batches and frozen.

name: Beet and Potato Salad
steps: Toss beets and potato with sour cream.
steps_detailed: Whisk the sour cream and vinegar together.
Toss the beets, potato, and red onion with the dressing - the beets will tint everything a deep pink, which is part of the dish's charm.
Fold in the dill before serving.
description: A simple beet and potato salad.

name: Cabbage and Egg Bowl
steps: Cook cabbage in a splash of water 8-10 minutes until tender.
Toss with chopped egg and sour cream.
steps_detailed: Melt the butter in a pan over medium heat and cook the cabbage 10-12 minutes, stirring occasionally, until tender and lightly golden at the edges.
Toss with the chopped egg and sour cream.
Top with dill before serving - a simple, comforting combination found throughout Russian home cooking.
description: A simple cabbage and egg dish.

name: Buckwheat Porridge (Kasha)
steps: Simmer rice (or buckwheat) in milk 15-18 minutes until tender and creamy.
Stir in butter.
steps_detailed: Combine the rice (or buckwheat groats, the traditional base) with the milk in a saucepan.
Bring to a gentle simmer and cook 15-20 minutes, stirring occasionally, until tender and the mixture has thickened.
Stir in the butter, honey, and cinnamon before serving warm - kasha is a foundational breakfast across Russia.
description: Kasha, buckwheat porridge, one of Russia's most fundamental staple foods.

name: Cottage Cheese Pancakes (Syrniki)
steps: Mix cottage cheese, egg, and flour into a batter.
Pan-fry small patties 2-3 minutes per side.
steps_detailed: Mix the cottage cheese, egg, sugar, and vanilla together, then fold in the flour just until combined - the batter should be soft but hold together.
Shape into small patties, dusting lightly with extra flour if too sticky to handle.
Heat oil in a skillet over medium heat and fry 2-3 minutes per side until golden.
Serve warm with a dollop of sour cream - syrniki are a beloved weekend breakfast across Russia.
description: Syrniki, farmer's-cheese pancakes, a beloved Russian breakfast.

name: Spiced Roasted Sunflower Seeds
steps: Toast in a dry skillet 3-4 minutes with salt.

ingredients_full.note: oz, or sunflower seeds if available

steps_detailed: Toss with oil and a pinch of salt.
Toast in a dry skillet over medium-low heat, 4-5 minutes, stirring often.
Cool before eating - roasted sunflower seeds ('semechki') are one of Russia's most iconic casual snacks, sold everywhere from train platforms to parks.
description: Sunflower seeds, an extremely popular snack across Russia.

name: Rye Bread with Butter and Dill
steps: Spread bread with butter, top with dill.
steps_detailed: Generously butter the rye bread.
Top with chopped dill and a light sprinkle of salt - a beloved, simple Russian snack, often served alongside soup or tea.
description: Rye bread, dense and dark, is Russia's everyday bread.

name: Pickled Vegetable Plate
steps: Serve sauerkraut and cucumber together.
steps_detailed: Arrange the sauerkraut and cucumber slices together on a plate.
Drizzle lightly with olive oil and scatter with dill - pickled and fermented vegetables are a constant presence on the Russian table, prized for cutting through richer dishes.
description: Pickled vegetables, a staple accompaniment across Russian meals.

name: Honey Cake Bites (Medovik-Style)
steps: Bake a simple honey-sweetened flour batter at 350F for 12-15 minutes.
Cool, layer with sour cream.
steps_detailed: Preheat the oven to 350F.
Melt the butter and honey together, then whisk in the egg and sugar.
Fold in the flour to form a soft batter, spread thin on a lined baking sheet.
Bake 12-15 minutes until golden, then cool completely and cut into small squares.
Layer or top with sour cream - a simplified take on medovik, the classic Russian honey layer cake.
description: Medovik, a layered honey cake, a beloved Russian dessert.

name: Chicken Cutlets (Kotleti-Style)
steps: Mix chicken with breadcrumbs, shape into patties.
Pan-fry 4-5 minutes per side.
Serve with mashed potato.
steps_detailed: Mix the ground chicken with grated onion, egg, and half the breadcrumbs until well combined.
Shape into oval patties, then coat lightly in the remaining breadcrumbs.
Heat oil in a skillet over medium heat and fry 4-5 minutes per side until golden and cooked through.
Mash the potato with butter and serve alongside - kotleti are a nightly staple across Russian households, endlessly variable but always comforting.
description: Kotleti, pan-fried ground meat cutlets, a Russian home-cooking staple.

name: Beef and Onion Skewers (Shashlik-Style)
steps: Marinate beef and onion in vinegar 15 minutes.
Skewer and grill 3-4 minutes per side.

ingredients_full.note: chunked, plus extra sliced for marinade

steps_detailed: Whisk the vinegar, garlic, oil, and black pepper together. Toss with the beef and marinate at least 30 minutes, or overnight for best flavor - onion is traditionally part of the marinade itself, not just a skewer filler.
Thread the beef and onion chunks onto skewers, alternating.
Grill over high heat, 3-4 minutes per side, until charred outside and cooked to preference.
Shashlik is a beloved grilling tradition, especially for gatherings at the dacha (country house).
description: Shashlik, grilled skewered meat, found across Russia and the broader former Soviet sphere.
